formatted_summary:
agent1 (Round 1 summarized): After a bilateral mandibular fracture near canines, muscles primarily responsible include the anterior belly of the digastric and thyrohyoid which elevate the hyoid bone, thereby tugging at the mandible backward; genioglossus is excluded due to its role projecting outward rather than retracting. Key takeaway involves identifying correct musculature affecting positional changes through their influence on adjacent structures after trauma. Agent1 reasoned based on anatomical functions where selected muscles act via elevating parts connected to the lower jaw, ensuring accurate identification despite complex interactions between various facial muscles involved in mastication processes. To summarize concisely while maintaining focus,

The primary muscles pulling the mandible back post-bilateral canine-region fracture are likely the anterior belly of the digastric and thyrohyoid since they raise the hyoid bone, whereas the genioglossus pulls downward without contributing significantly to retroversion. Understanding muscular anatomy’s impact helps predict functional outcomes accurately amidst craniofacial injuries. \boxed{B}

In summary,
- Muscles raising the hyoid bone (digastric & thyrohyoid) cause mandibular retrusion.
- Genioglossus doesn't contribute substantially here due to different function. 
Key takeaways revolve around correctly matching affected musculature influencing bony position change post-trauma. \boxed{B}. Answer choice: B
